From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Internet top-level domain generally used by or reserved for a country

"ccTLD" and "cc TLD" redirect here. For the TLD for the Cocos (Keeling) Islands, see [.cc](/wiki/.cc ".cc").

For a list of TLDs, see [List of Internet top-level domains](/wiki/List_of_Internet_top-level_domains "List of Internet top-level domains").

A **country code top-level domain** (**ccTLD**) is an [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") [top-level domain](/wiki/Top-level_domain "Top-level domain") generally used or reserved for a [country](/wiki/Country "Country"), sovereign state, or [dependent territory](/wiki/Dependent_territory "Dependent territory") identified with a [country code](/wiki/Country_code "Country code"). All [ASCII](/wiki/ASCII "ASCII") ccTLD identifiers are two letters long, and all two-letter top-level domains are ccTLDs.

In 2018, the [Internet Assigned Numbers Authority](/wiki/Internet_Assigned_Numbers_Authority "Internet Assigned Numbers Authority") (IANA) began implementing [internationalized country code top-level domains](/wiki/Internationalized_country_code_top-level_domain "Internationalized country code top-level domain"), consisting of language-native characters when displayed in an end-user application. Creation and delegation of ccTLDs is described in RFC 1591, corresponding to [ISO 3166-1 alpha-2](/wiki/ISO_3166-1_alpha-2 "ISO 3166-1 alpha-2") [country codes](/wiki/Country_codes "Country codes"). While [gTLDs](/wiki/GTLD "GTLD") have to obey international regulations, ccTLDs are subjected to requirements that are determined by each country’s domain name regulation corporation. With over 150 million domain name registrations today or as of 2022, ccTLDs make up about 40% of the total domain name industry.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Country code extension applications began in 1985. The registered country code extensions in that year included [.us](/wiki/.us ".us") (United States), [.uk](/wiki/.uk ".uk") (United Kingdom) and [.il](/wiki/.il ".il") (Israel). The registered country code extensions in 1986 included [.au](/wiki/.au ".au") (Australia), [.de](/wiki/.de ".de") (Germany), [.fi](/wiki/.fi ".fi") (Finland), [.fr](/wiki/.fr ".fr") (France), [.is](/wiki/.is ".is") (Iceland), [.jp](/wiki/.jp ".jp") (Japan), [.kr](/wiki/.kr ".kr") (South Korea), [.nl](/wiki/.nl ".nl") (Netherlands) and [.se](/wiki/.se ".se") (Sweden). The registered country code extensions in 1987 included [.nz](/wiki/.nz ".nz") (New Zealand), [.ch](/wiki/.ch ".ch") (Switzerland) and [.ca](/wiki/.ca ".ca") (Canada).[[2]](#cite_note-2) The registered country code extensions in 1988 included [.ie](/wiki/.ie ".ie") (Ireland) [.it](/wiki/.it ".it") (Italy), [.es](/wiki/.es ".es") (Spain) and [.pt](/wiki/.pt ".pt") (Portugal). The registered country code extensions in 1989 included [.in](/wiki/.in ".in") (India) and [.yu](/wiki/.yu ".yu") (Yugoslavia). In the 1990s, [.cn](/wiki/.cn ".cn") (People’s Republic of China) and [.ru](/wiki/.ru ".ru") (Russian Federation) were first registered.

There are over 300 delegated ccTLDs. The [.cn](/wiki/.cn ".cn"), [.tk](/wiki/.tk ".tk"), [.de](/wiki/.de ".de"), [.uk](/wiki/.uk ".uk"), [.nl](/wiki/.nl ".nl") and [.ru](/wiki/.ru ".ru") ccTLDs contain the highest number of domains[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. The top ten ccTLDs account for more than five-eighths of registered ccTLD domains[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. There were about 153 million ccTLD domains registered at the end of March 2022.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Delegation and management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Country_code_top-level_domain&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Delegation and management")]

IANA is responsible for determining an appropriate trustee for each ccTLD. Administration and control are then delegated to that trustee, which is responsible for the policies and operation of the domain. The current delegation can be determined from IANA's list of ccTLDs.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Individual ccTLDs may have varying requirements and fees for registering [subdomains](/wiki/Subdomains "Subdomains"). There may be a local-presence requirement (for instance, citizenship or other connection to the ccTLD), as, for example, the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") ([us](/wiki/.us ".us")), [Japanese](/wiki/Japan "Japan") ([jp](/wiki/.jp ".jp")), [Canadian](/wiki/Canada "Canada") ([ca](/wiki/.ca ".ca")), [French](/wiki/France "France") ([fr](/wiki/.fr ".fr")) and [German](/wiki/Germany "Germany") ([de](/wiki/.de ".de")) domains, or registration may be open.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Country_code_top-level_domain&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The first registered ccTLD was [.us](/wiki/.us ".us"), which was registered in 1985. Later ccTLDs registered in that year included [.uk](/wiki/.uk ".uk") and [.il](/wiki/.il ".il"). Then, [.au](/wiki/.au ".au"), [.de](/wiki/.de ".de"), [.fi](/wiki/.fi ".fi"), [.fr](/wiki/.fr ".fr"), [.is](/wiki/.is ".is"), [.jp](/wiki/.jp ".jp"), [.kr](/wiki/.kr ".kr"), [.nl](/wiki/.nl ".nl") and [.se](/wiki/.se ".se") were also registered in 1986.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 1987, [.nz](/wiki/.nz ".nz"), [.ch](/wiki/.ch ".ch"), [.my](/wiki/.my ".my") [.ca](/wiki/.ca ".ca") were registered. Later on, in 1988, [.ie](/wiki/.ie ".ie"), [.it](/wiki/.it ".it"), [.es](/wiki/.es ".es") and [.pt](/wiki/.pt ".pt") were also registered.

## Lists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Country_code_top-level_domain&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Lists")]

As of 20 May 2017, there were 255 country-code top-level domains, purely in the Latin alphabet, using two-character codes. The number was 316 as of June 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Country_code_top-level_domain&action=edit), with the addition of internationalized domains.[[6]](#cite_note-iana-6)

## Relation to ISO 3166-1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Country_code_top-level_domain&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Relation to ISO 3166-1")]

> The IANA is not in the business of deciding what is and what is not a country. The selection of the ISO 3166 list as a basis for country code top-level domain names was made with the knowledge that ISO has a procedure for determining which entities should be and should not be on that list.

— [Jon Postel](/wiki/Jon_Postel "Jon Postel"), RFC 1591[[85]](#cite_note-105)

### Unused ISO 3166-1 codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Country_code_top-level_domain&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Unused ISO 3166-1 codes")]

Almost all current ISO 3166-1 codes have been assigned and do exist in DNS.
However, some of these are effectively unused. In particular, the ccTLDs for the Norwegian dependency [Bouvet Island](/wiki/Bouvet_Island "Bouvet Island") (`bv`) and the designation [Svalbard and Jan Mayen](/wiki/Svalbard_and_Jan_Mayen "Svalbard and Jan Mayen") (`sj`) do exist in DNS, but no subdomains have been assigned, and it is [Norid](/wiki/Norid "Norid") policy to not assign any at present. Two [French](/wiki/France "France") territories—`bl` ([Saint Barthélemy](/wiki/Saint_Barth%C3%A9lemy "Saint Barthélemy")) and `mf` ([Saint Martin](/wiki/Saint_Martin_(France) "Saint Martin (France)"))—still[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Country_code_top-level_domain&action=edit) await local assignment by France's government.

The code `eh`, although eligible as ccTLD for [Western Sahara](/wiki/Western_Sahara "Western Sahara"), has never been assigned and does not exist in [DNS](/wiki/Domain_Name_System "Domain Name System"). Only one subdomain is still registered in `gb`[[86]](#cite_note-106) (ISO 3166-1 for the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")), and no new registrations are being accepted for it. Sites in the United Kingdom generally use `uk` (see below).

The former [.um](/wiki/.um ".um") ccTLD for the [U.S. Minor Outlying Islands](/wiki/United_States_Minor_Outlying_Islands "United States Minor Outlying Islands") was removed in April 2008. Under RFC 1591 rules, [.um](/wiki/.um ".um") is eligible as a ccTLD on request by the relevant governmental agency and local Internet user community.

### ASCII ccTLDs not in ISO 3166-1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Country_code_top-level_domain&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: ASCII ccTLDs not in ISO 3166-1")]

Several ASCII ccTLDs are in use that are not ISO 3166-1 two-letter codes. Some of these codes were specified in older versions of the ISO list.

* `uk` ([United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")): The ISO 3166-1 code for the United Kingdom is GB. However, the [JANET](/wiki/JANET "JANET") network had already selected `uk` as a top-level identifier for its pre-existing [Name Registration Scheme](/wiki/JANET_NRS "JANET NRS"), and this was incorporated into the DNS root. `gb` was assigned with the intention of a transition, but this never occurred and the use of `uk` is now entrenched.[[87]](#cite_note-Mueller2002-107)
* `su` This obsolete ISO 3166 code for the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") was assigned when the Soviet Union still existed; moreover, new `su` registrations are accepted.
* `ac` ([Ascension Island](/wiki/Ascension_Island "Ascension Island")): This code is a vestige of [IANA's](/wiki/Internet_Assigned_Numbers_Authority "Internet Assigned Numbers Authority") decision in 1996 to allow the use of codes reserved in the [ISO 3166-1 alpha-2](/wiki/ISO_3166-1_alpha-2 "ISO 3166-1 alpha-2") reserve list for use by the [Universal Postal Union](/wiki/Universal_Postal_Union "Universal Postal Union"). The decision was later reversed, with Ascension Island now the sole outlier. (Three other ccTLDs, `gg` ([Guernsey](/wiki/Bailiwick_of_Guernsey "Bailiwick of Guernsey")), `im` ([Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man")) and `je` ([Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey")) also fell under this category from 1996 until they received corresponding ISO 3166 codes in March 2006.)
* `eu` ([European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union")): On September 25, 2000, [ICANN](/wiki/ICANN "ICANN") decided to allow the use of any two-letter code in the [ISO 3166-1](/wiki/ISO_3166-1 "ISO 3166-1") reserve list that is reserved for all purposes. Only EU currently meets this criterion. Following a decision by the EU's Council of Telecommunications Ministers in March 2002, progress was slow, but a [registry](/wiki/Domain_name_registry "Domain name registry") (named [EURid](/wiki/EURid "EURid")) was chosen by the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"), and criteria for allocation set: [ICANN](/wiki/ICANN "ICANN") approved `eu` as a ccTLD, and it opened for registration on 7 December 2005 for the holders of prior rights. Since 7 April 2006, registration is open to all in the [European Economic Area](/wiki/European_Economic_Area "European Economic Area").

### Historical ccTLDs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Country_code_top-level_domain&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Historical ccTLDs")]

ccTLDs may be removed if that country ceases to exist. There are three ccTLDs that have been deleted after the corresponding 2-letter code was withdrawn from ISO 3166-1: `cs` (for [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia")), `zr` (for [Zaire](/wiki/Zaire "Zaire")) and `tp` (for [East Timor](/wiki/East_Timor "East Timor")). There may be a significant delay between withdrawal from ISO 3166-1 and deletion from the DNS; for example, ZR ceased to be an ISO 3166-1 code in 1997, but the `zr` ccTLD was not deleted until 2001. Other ccTLDs corresponding to obsolete ISO 3166-1 codes have not yet been deleted. In some cases they may never be deleted due to the amount of disruption this would cause for a heavily used ccTLD. In particular, the Soviet Union's ccTLD `su` remains in use more than twenty years after SU was removed from ISO 3166-1.

The historical country codes `dd` for the [German Democratic Republic](/wiki/German_Democratic_Republic "German Democratic Republic") and `yd` for [South Yemen](/wiki/South_Yemen "South Yemen") were eligible for a ccTLD, but not allocated; see also `de` and `ye`.

The temporary reassignment of country code `cs` ([Serbia and Montenegro](/wiki/Serbia_and_Montenegro "Serbia and Montenegro")) until its split into `rs` and `me` ([Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") and [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro "Montenegro"), respectively) led to some controversies[[88]](#cite_note-108)[[89]](#cite_note-109) about the stability of ISO 3166-1 country codes, resulting in a second edition of ISO 3166-1 in 2007 with a guarantee that retired codes will not be reassigned for at least 50 years, and the replacement of RFC 3066 by RFC 4646 for country codes used in [language tags](/wiki/Language_tag "Language tag") in 2006.

The previous ISO 3166-1 code for [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia"), YU, was removed by ISO on 23 July 2003, but the `yu` ccTLD remained in operation. Finally, after a two-year transition to Serbian `rs` and Montenegrin `me`, the .yu domain was phased out in March 2010.

Australia was originally assigned the `oz` country code, which was later changed to `au` with the `.oz` domains moved to `.oz.au`.

## Internationalized ccTLDs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Country_code_top-level_domain&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Internationalized ccTLDs")]

An [internationalized country code top-level domain](/wiki/Internationalized_country_code_top-level_domain "Internationalized country code top-level domain") (IDN ccTLD) is a top-level domain with a specially encoded domain name that is displayed in an end user application, such as a [web browser](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser"), in its native language script or a non-alphabetic [writing system](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system"), such as [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script") (.us, .uk and .br), [Indic script](/wiki/Indic_script "Indic script") (.भारत) and [Korean script](/wiki/Korean_script "Korean script") (.한국), etc. IDN ccTLDs are an application of the [internationalized domain name](/wiki/Internationalized_domain_name "Internationalized domain name") (IDN) system to top-level Internet domains assigned to countries, including the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), or independent geographic regions.

ICANN started to accept applications for IDN ccTLDs in November 2009,[[90]](#cite_note-110) and installed the first set into the Domain Names System in May 2010. The first set was a group of Arabic names for the countries of Egypt, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates. By May 2010, 21 countries had submitted applications to ICANN, representing 11 languages.[[91]](#cite_note-111)

ICANN requires all potential international TLDs to use at least one letter that does not resemble a Latin letter, or have at least three letters, in an effort to avoid [IDN homograph attacks](/wiki/IDN_homograph_attack "IDN homograph attack"). Nor shall the international domain name look like another domain name, even if they have different alphabets. Between Cyrillic and Greek alphabets, for example, this could happen.

## Unconventional usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Country_code_top-level_domain&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Unconventional usage")]

Main article: [Vanity URL](/wiki/Vanity_URL "Vanity URL")

Lenient registration restrictions on certain ccTLDs have resulted in various [domain hacks](/wiki/Domain_hack "Domain hack"). Domain names such as `I.am`, `tip.it`, `start.at` and `go.to` form well-known English phrases, whereas others combine the [second-level domain](/wiki/Second-level_domain "Second-level domain") and ccTLD to form one word or one title, creating domains such as `blo.gs` of [South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands](/wiki/South_Georgia_and_the_South_Sandwich_Islands "South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands") (`gs`), `youtu.be` of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") (`be`), `del.icio.us` of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") (`us`), and `cr.yp.to` of [Tonga](/wiki/Tonga "Tonga") (`to`). The `.co` domain of Colombia has been cited since 2010 as a potential competitor to generic TLDs for commercial use, because it may be an abbreviation for *company*.[[95]](#cite_note-unique-115)

Several ccTLDs allow the creation of [emoji domains](/wiki/Emoji_domain "Emoji domain").

Some ccTLDs may also be used for [typosquatting](/wiki/Typosquatting "Typosquatting"). The domain *cm* of [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon") has generated interest due to the possibility that people might miss typing the letter *o* for sites in the *com*.[[96]](#cite_note-116)

### Commercial use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Country_code_top-level_domain&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Commercial use")]

Some of the world's smallest countries and non-sovereign or colonial entities with their own country codes have opened their TLDs for worldwide commercial use, some of them free like [.tk](/wiki/.tk ".tk").
